findings.message: DomainService nodes are emitted with the `:::domain_service` class, but the renderer header only defines classDefs for secondary_adapter, command, query, factory, and free_function. This leaves the new class reference undefined in generated Mermaid output, unlike every other styled kind; add a `classDef domain_service ...` line and cover it in the shape/class test.

DomainService nodes are emitted with the `:::domain_service` class, but `render_contract_map` only declares classDefs for `secondary_adapter`, `command`, `query`, `factory`, and `free_function`. This leaves the new Mermaid class reference undefined in generated contract maps; add a `classDef domain_service ...` header entry and cover the DomainService shape/class in the renderer test.

The SecondaryAdapter variant documentation says renderer `methods_of()` merges direct `expected_methods` with `implements[].expected_methods`, but the current renderer arm only reads `implements` and ignores the new top-level `expected_methods`. Since T003 is explicitly deferred, this comment is currently a factual contradiction and should be reworded as future behavior or the renderer should actually merge both sources.

The task requires `contract_map_render.rs` to stay within `architecture-rules.json` `module_limits.max_lines = 700`, but this patch adds the new tests directly to the same file. The file is now 1797 lines, and the module-size verifier counts full `.rs` files without excluding `#[cfg(test)]`, so the line-limit acceptance criterion remains unsatisfied. Move the tests into a separate test module/file or split the renderer before marking this done.

S1 describes `domain_service` as the place for DDD domain services, including stateless aggregate-crossing operations, but the selection rules only admit structs with at least one field and route zero-field cases to `free_function`. As written, the new kind does not actually cover the stateless domain-service case it claims to solve.

S2 says the current `methods_of()` special handling becomes unnecessary, but the replacement still keeps direct arms for `SecondaryPort` and `ApplicationService` and preserves the `SecondaryAdapter` trait-impl path. This is a simplification, not elimination, so the claim is overstated.

`field empty enforcement` for `value_object.expected_methods` would make every `value_object` method illegal, including the `new() -> Result<Self, FooError>` constructors this ADR says M1 is meant to enable. If getters/accessors are still allowed, the rule needs to target a narrower notion of behavior than "all methods".

The `SecondaryAdapter` renderer branch still reads only `implements[].expected_methods`, so the new direct `expected_methods` added to `SecondaryAdapter` in M1 would never affect contract-map rendering or validation. That leaves one of the introduced schema fields effectively dead.

S1 describes `domain_service` as a stateless domain service, but the same section makes it a field-bearing kind (`expected_members`) and then requires at least one member in the selection criteria. That leaves the new kind's semantics inconsistent: readers cannot tell whether it is meant for pure stateless services or stateful behavioral structs.

M1 says all nine struct kinds gain `expected_methods`, but the proposed `methods_of()` rewrite still excludes `Typestate` from the uniform match arm. As written, one of the supposedly uniformized struct kinds remains on the old non-method path, so the renderer change does not actually match the decision text.

S2 says `methods_of()` will be made uniform after M1, but the proposed match still special-cases `SecondaryAdapter` via `implements` only. That leaves any methods declared on `SecondaryAdapter.expected_methods` invisible to the contract-map, so the renderer change does not actually match the schema uniformization promised in M1.

The new ADR is listed in the README index with status `—`, but the ADR frontmatter marks all decisions as `proposed`; this leaves the index out of sync with the artifact's lifecycle state. Use an explicit lifecycle state here instead of `—`.

S2 says `methods_of()` will use "uniform 処理 (特例分岐なし)" and that the existing special branches become unnecessary, but the proposed implementation still gives `SecondaryAdapter` its own branch that merges top-level `expected_methods` with `implements[].expected_methods`. That leaves the design intent ambiguous: either `SecondaryAdapter` is intentionally special and the surrounding text/consequences should stop promising no special case, or the implementation sketch should handle it uniformly like the other struct kinds.

§4 treats the `ComplianceContext`-class behavioral struct not appearing in the graph as a downstream effect of `methods_of()`/schema non-uniformity, but later the ADR states that the current `ComplianceContext` has no catalogue declaration and will be authored in another track. An undeclared type cannot be made visible by renderer/schema uniformization, so the ADR conflates missing catalogue coverage with missing method-edge rendering and overstates what M1/S2 will resolve.

`domain_service` の配置方針が矛盾しています。S1 では placement table と判定基準で usecase 層配置を「trans-domain な application logic の場合のみ要根拠」として許容している一方、S3 の linter primitive 例では `domain_service` を「domain 層のみ」として enforce するとしています。このままだと実装者が linter を domain-only にすべきか、usecase 条件付き許容にすべきか判断できません。

Line 22 combines `#[serde(default)]` with “JSON の後方互換は持たない”. A defaulted field will still deserialize pre-change JSON that omits `expected_methods`, so the plan is asking for backward compatibility and forbidding it at the same time. Pick one behavior and align the codec task with it.

Lines 48-57 require `KindLayerConstraint` / `InvalidLayer`, but no task passes layer context into `CatalogueLinter::run` or provides another layer registry source. The adapter only sees `&TypeCatalogueDocument`, and the interactor only injects `CatalogueLoader` + `CatalogueLinter`, so this primitive cannot be evaluated as written. Add an explicit layer context to the port or move the check into the loader/interactor before this can be implemented.
Line 22 mixes `#[serde(default)]` with a no-backward-compat requirement. A defaulted field will still accept pre-change JSON missing `expected_methods`, so the codec requirement contradicts itself.

T005/T006 define `KindLayerConstraint`, but the port only receives `&TypeCatalogueDocument` and the interactor never passes any layer registry or layer context into `run`. As written, this primitive has no source of truth for permitted-layer checks.

The `CatalogueLinterRule` description claims the validating constructor rejects unknown kind tags, but T004 only models empty-string/layer validation and the error shape has no way to represent an unknown target kind. That validation promise is not supported by the plan.

T004 defines `CatalogueLinter::run` without `layer_id`, but the rest of the plan models the same port with `layer_id: &str` and T005/T006 are written against that 3-argument signature. As written, the plan gives two incompatible interfaces for `CatalogueLinter`, so the adapter and interactor tasks cannot be implemented consistently until T004 is aligned.
`contract-map.md` is missing the required `DO NOT EDIT DIRECTLY` generated-file marker, which contradicts CN-08 and the same convention preserved in the other rendered markdown artifacts.

The InMemoryCatalogueLinter entry says rule opt-out via `disabled_rules` is supported, and T005 requires the adapter to hold `disabled_rules: Vec<String>` so disabled rules suppress violations, but the catalogue contract declares `expected_members: []`. An implementer following this artifact can build a stateless adapter and miss the CN-06 opt-out requirement.

The usecase interactor description injects `FsCatalogueLoader`, a concrete infrastructure adapter, even though the same entry declares `catalogue_loader: Arc<dyn CatalogueLoader>` and T007 says the CLI composition root wires `FsCatalogueLoader`. This contradicts the intended trait boundary and can lead the usecase layer to depend on infrastructure.

RunCatalogueLintInteractor is described as injecting the concrete `FsCatalogueLoader`, while the same catalogue entry declares `catalogue_loader: Arc<dyn CatalogueLoader>` and the implementation plan places `FsCatalogueLoader` wiring in the CLI composition root. This contradicts the trait boundary for the usecase layer and can lead implementation toward an infrastructure dependency.

InMemoryCatalogueLinter is required to support rule opt-out via `disabled_rules`, and T005 says the adapter holds `disabled_rules: Vec<String>`, but the catalogue contract declares `expected_members: []`. An implementer following this artifact can build a stateless adapter and miss the CN-06 opt-out requirement.

RunCatalogueLintInteractor is described as injecting the concrete `FsCatalogueLoader`, while the same catalogue entry declares `catalogue_loader: Arc<dyn CatalogueLoader>` and the implementation plan places `FsCatalogueLoader` wiring in the CLI composition root. This contradicts the trait boundary for the usecase layer and can lead implementation toward an infrastructure dependency.

M1/CN-01 requires every struct-based kind to carry `expected_methods`, and T002 explicitly makes the new codec reject missing `expected_methods` by omitting `#[serde(default)]`. However the newly authored catalogue entries still use the pre-M1 shape; for example `CatalogueLinterRule` is a `value_object` with `expected_members` only. The same omission appears on `CatalogueLintViolation`, `RunCatalogueLintCommand`, `RunCatalogueLintInteractor`, and `InMemoryCatalogueLinter`. After T002, these artifacts become invalid unless the plan updates them to include empty/top-level `expected_methods`.

T006 describes the usecase interactor as depending only on domain ports (`Arc<dyn CatalogueLoader>` and `Arc<dyn CatalogueLinter>`) and using mocks in tests, but the same task declares T005, the infrastructure adapter, as a prerequisite. That contradicts the port-based layer boundary and the intended composition-root split where T007 wires concrete adapters; T006 should be unblocked after T004, while T007 should depend on both T005 and T006.

`CatalogueLinter` is a `secondary_port`, but this artifact adds `expected_methods` to it. The scope policy explicitly says `secondary_port` entries must not gain that field, so this JSON no longer matches the intended plan-artifact schema.

`RunCatalogueLint` is an `application_service`, but this artifact adds `expected_methods` to it. The scope policy explicitly excludes `application_service` entries from the new field, so this entry is semantically inconsistent with the intended codec/catalogue shape.

DomainService placement is described inconsistently: this task text says `domain/usecase ✓`, but the surrounding S1/S8 matrix says `usecase △`. The plan needs one consistent rule for whether usecase placement is fully allowed or only conditional.

T004 is now marked in progress and the plan describes the six linter foundation types as the active deliverable, but the rendered domain type view only refreshes four of them to 🔵 and leaves `CatalogueLinterRule` / `CatalogueLintViolation` at 🟡. That makes the plan artifact internally inconsistent about whether the T004 domain types have been detected, so readers and gates see a partial signal refresh for a single six-type task.

`load_all(track_id)` is described here as if it fetches the layer identified by `layer_id`, but the call is keyed by `track_id` and `layer_id` is only used later for validation/linting. As written, the interactor contract conflates the track scope with the layer scope.

`DomainService` entries still will not render: `render_type_catalogue` only iterates entries whose `kind_tag()` matches a tag in `SECTIONS`, and this list has no `domain_service` section. The new `render_details` arm is therefore unreachable for `DomainService`, and the existing coverage test also misses it because its sample list omits `TypeDefinitionKind::DomainService`. Any in-memory catalogue containing a domain service will silently drop that row from the generated markdown.

DomainService is a new struct-based kind, but it is not included in the Phase 1.5 forbidden-field guard. A `domain_service` entry with `expected_methods` plus a stale field such as `implements`, `expected_variants`, or `transitions_to` will pass this pre-validation and serde will silently drop the stale field during flattened enum deserialization, reintroducing the data-loss case this guard is meant to prevent. Add `domain_service` to this guard or add an equivalent DomainService-specific check.

Invalid `target_field` values are only validated inside the per-entry loop after `target_kind` matches. If a bad FieldEmpty/FieldNonEmpty rule targets a kind that is absent from the catalogue, `run()` returns `Ok([])` instead of `CatalogueLinterError::InvalidRuleConfig`, contrary to the documented unknown-field error behavior. Validate field-rule target fields once per rule before scanning entries.

Step 1 offers "continue on the current branch" when the starting branch is not `main`, but Step 2 still unconditionally creates `track/<id>` from `main`. That leaves no actual execution path for the suggested non-main continuation option, so the command can send the user into an impossible choice.

The non-empty `git status` handling is internally inconsistent for the intended baseline-file case: it first says every listed item needs user handling, then says `knowledge/` baseline files need no action and can just be carried to Step 3, while still requiring the user to resolve the non-empty state before proceeding. As written, the common baseline case can still block the bootstrap flow without a clear resolution rule.

Step 3 tells users to run `/track:review` → `/track:commit` immediately after branch creation, but `/track:review` reads the current track's `metadata.json` before it can proceed. At that point step 4 has not created `track/items/<track-id>/metadata.json` yet, so the documented 'typical' flow is not actually executable in this order.

Step 3 tells users to run `/track:review` → `/track:commit` immediately after branch creation, but `/track:review`’s own context-gathering step requires the track’s `metadata.json` to already exist. Because `metadata.json` is only created in step 4, the documented first-commit path is not executable in the order shown.

The step sequence no longer matches the harness-policy intent: step 3 is now `metadata.json` creation, step 4 is verification, and the `/track:review` → `/track:commit` guidance is pushed to step 5. That makes the renumbered workflow inconsistent with the requested 1-5 ordering and leaves the earlier `step 5 below` reference pointing at a different kind of action than the briefing intended.

Step 5 says that having `metadata.json` 'satisfies `/track:review`'s prerequisite of reading `metadata.json` / `plan.md` / `spec.md`', but this init flow only creates `metadata.json`. `plan.md` and `spec.md` are not produced here, so the command is asserting prerequisite satisfaction that it does not actually establish.

Step 5 sends the user directly from metadata verification to `/track:review` and says `plan.md` is auto-rendered, but steps 1-4 never run `cargo make track-sync-views`. A freshly initialized track can therefore reach the first-review flow with only `metadata.json` present, while the review command expects `plan.md`/track context before invoking the reviewer. Add an explicit `track-sync-views` step before the `/track:review` guidance, or make the guidance point to a command path that performs the sync first.

Broadening `completed` to mean “zero_findings for the assigned round_type” is incompatible with the caller contract in `.claude/commands/track/review.md:199-234`, which still treats `completed` as the fast-round handoff that triggers step 2f full-model confirmation. If a final-round agent reports `completed`, the orchestrator will mis-handle it as another fast completion and re-enter confirmation instead of terminating.

Step 2.5 overstates what `--round-type` does. The `track/review.md` docs say the filter only applies to displayed history, while the state-line summary still reflects the newest round regardless of the filter. As written, an agent can still misread the unfiltered summary and treat the wrong round as the completion verdict.

`completed` now means “the assigned round_type returned zero_findings,” but the orchestrator flow in `.claude/commands/track/review.md` still treats `completed` as a fast-round-only signal and immediately launches final confirmation. A successful `final` round would therefore re-trigger confirmation instead of terminating the scope, so the escalation loop does not converge.

The agent definition still includes a parenthetical incident reference, `over-long review loops (28-round history)`, despite the harness-policy checklist requiring the body to be rule/step-list only with past incident references removed. This leaves the requested policy cleanup incomplete.

Step 2.5 tells the agent to verify an inline entry containing `verdict: zero_findings`, but the referenced `track-review-results --limit 1` output does not emit a `verdict:` field for the displayed round; the current API renders the filtered latest round as `findings: zero_findings` or a findings list, while only history lines contain `<round>@<timestamp> <verdict>`. This makes the mandatory completion check look for a string the canonical API does not print, so an agent can fail to ever report `completed` after a successful review.

Step 2 removes the `track-review-results` flag reference section, but `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md:81-82` still points readers at that subsection for the complete option list. That leaves a dangling cross-document reference in the review workflow documentation.

Step 0 dropped the branchless planning-only guard and the legacy `plan.md` fallback in the same rewrite. That leaves `/track:review` on `plan/<id>` branches without the documented activation gate that keeps code-bearing diffs out of the review path, and it no longer loads required conventions for legacy tracks that still rely on `plan.md` instead of `spec.md`.

Step 4 says the agent prompt must include the scope file list, but the only mechanism it documents is auto-injection into the reviewer subprocess. `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md` still treats that file list as direct orchestrator input, so this prompt no longer explains how to satisfy the agent contract or enforce the scope boundary from the orchestrator side.

Step 5 is internally inconsistent with the review-fix-lead contract: it says the orchestrator should react to 'final returns new findings' and relaunch based on the finding nature, but this file and `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md` both describe the agent as handling review→fix→re-review internally and only reporting `completed`/`blocked_cross_scope`/`failed`. That makes the final-findings branch unreachable and leaves no documented path for the orchestrator to obtain those findings.

Step 0 now only tells the reviewer to read conventions listed in `spec.md`, but legacy tracks still keep `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` in `plan.md` instead (for example `track/items/track-plan-activation-2026-03-14/plan.md`). On those tracks, `/track:review` will skip mandatory convention docs and can review against incomplete requirements.

Step 4 now says the review-fix-lead agent resolves its modification boundary from `track/review-scope.json` glob patterns intersected with the current diff, but the `other` scope is implicit and has no group patterns in that config. Since Step 2 can still mark `other` as required, the orchestrator no longer provides any concrete owned-file list for that agent, leaving it unable to distinguish in-scope fixes from cross-scope edits. This breaks the per-scope review loop for unmatched changed files.

Step 4 no longer tells the orchestrator to include the scope file list in the `review-fix-lead` prompt, even though the agent contract requires that list as its modification boundary. This is especially broken for the `other` scope: the new text says the CLI-injected file list is authoritative, but the listed minimum prompt only passes a briefing path and reviewer invocation, so the fix agent has no reliable pre-fix boundary for files that have no `review-scope.json` patterns. An `other`-scope review could therefore fail closed or edit out of scope. Add the scope file list back to the required agent prompt content, or specify the exact canonical command/output the agent must use to obtain it before fixing.

Step 4 defines the `other` agent boundary as the full changed file list minus named-group and `review_operational` matches, but the actual scope classifier excludes `other_track` patterns before assigning files to `Other` (`track/items/<other-track>/**`, `track/archive/**`). If a diff contains real `other` files plus another track/archive file, the orchestrator prompt would authorize the review-fix-lead agent to modify files that the CLI did not classify into the `other` scope and that are not part of the reviewed target, breaking the scope-ownership contract. The `other` boundary needs to subtract `other_track` matches as well, with the existing current-track exception handled by the classifier logic.

Step 4 defines named-scope ownership as all changed files matching the group's glob patterns, but the CLI classifier excludes `review_operational` and `other_track` files before assigning any named scope. For `plan-artifacts`, whose pattern includes `track/items/<track-id>/**`, this prompt would authorize the agent to modify operational files like `review.json` or type-signal outputs that the CLI does not classify or hash into that scope, breaking the scope-ownership contract. The named-scope file list should be computed with the same exclusion layers as the CLI classifier.

Step 5 asks the orchestrator to react to 'final returns new findings' by updating the briefing and relaunching, but `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md` defines the agent as internally looping until it reports `completed`, `blocked_cross_scope`, or `failed`, and Step 4 says the orchestrator does not parse reviewer JSON directly. There is no documented path for the orchestrator to observe intermediate final-round findings, so this branch is unreachable and the escalation flow is incomplete.

Removing the `### 2b. Build per-group briefing` anchor breaks existing source traces that still point at `review.md §2b. Build per-group briefing` (for example `track/items/review-scope-prompt-injection-2026-04-18/spec.md:28` and its metadata). Those docs now reference a nonexistent section name.

The `track-review-results flag reference` subsection is no longer a complete option list, but `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md:81-82` still tells agents to use it as one. The new text only lists a few common flags and omits supported options such as `--all` and `--no-hint`, so the cross-doc contract is now false.

`bin/sotp review results --help` is not a usable lookup path under this repo's permission policy, which denies direct `bin/sotp` invocations when a `cargo make` wrapper exists. That makes the 'complete option list' instruction a dead end for agents following the documented workflow; point this at an allowed wrapper/source instead.

Step 5 is unreachable as written: it asks the orchestrator to react when a `final` round “returns new findings”, but `.claude/agents/review-fix-lead.md` only reports `completed` / `blocked_cross_scope` / `failed`, and Step 4 explicitly says the orchestrator does not parse reviewer JSON directly. There is no documented way to observe `findings_remain` from the final round, so the relaunch branch cannot actually run.

The scope-file-list recipe excludes every `other_track` match before keeping named-group files, but the v2 classifier keeps current-track `track/items/<track-id>/**` files even when they match an `other_track` glob. As written, current-track `plan-artifacts` files can be dropped from the agent allowlist, which breaks the modification boundary for that scope.

Step 4 still tells the orchestrator to derive and inject the per-scope file list from `track/review-scope.json`, but the CLI already appends that list inside `CodexReviewer::build_full_prompt` (`apps/cli/src/commands/review/codex_local.rs`). That leaves two sources of truth for scope ownership and reintroduces manual classification drift that Step 2 says the CLI owns.

The opening summary says `/track:review` should not allow commit until every required scope reaches `final` round `zero_findings`, but Step 6 documents the supported `NotStarted` bypass for PR-only reviews (`review.json` absent and all required scopes `NotStarted`), which lets `track-check-approved` exit 0 without any final round. The summary overstates the gate for a valid workflow path.
